general terms.def: Terms that are not specific to Unani medicine, used in Unani medicine literature

astronomy.def: Science which deals with the movement of celestial bodies

matter. A science which deals with the study of sun, moon, stars and galaxies and their effects on earth and human life. Its possible English equivalent is astronomy. When two or more established facts are

intellect.def: Faculty perceiving self-evident things and meanings,independent of matter in its existence and function, through which mind perceives self-evident things and meanings in their complete sense

rational soul/ human soul.def: Soul which coordinates rational functions of the human; this term does not cover Nafs Nāṭiqa mentioned under factors of existence

movement.def: This term does not cover Ḥarakat mentioned under factors of existence

positional movement.def: A type of movement accompanied by change in place of body or object, partially or totally

axial movement.def: A type of movement accompanied by a change in position of body or object, but without changing its place

quantitative movement.def: A type of movement accompanied by a change in quantity of body or object Description

qualitative movement.def: A type of movement accompanied by qualitative change in body or object

three kingdoms of nature.def: Kingdoms of nature found as plants, animals and minerals

equator.def: An imaginary line which passes from east to west and divides globe into equal north and south hemispheres

celestial circle.def: An imaginary celestial circle which lies on same plane as equator of the earth; the day and night become equal when sun comes across this plane

imaginary illustration of celestial orbi.def: An imaginary illustration of celestial orbit divided into twelve divisions

school of thought supporting experience.def: One of three schools of thought having difference of opinion on the method of obtaining useful things for health, stating that such things are acquired through experience

school of thought supporting assumption.def: One of three schools of thought having difference of opinion on method of obtaining useful things for health, stating that experience alone is not sufficient and must be combined with syllogism to obtain such things

school of thought not supporting views o.def: One of three schools of thought having difference of opinion on method of obtaining useful things for health, summarizing science of medicine through manipulations by removing topics with which Aṣḥāb al-Tajriba and Aṣḥāb

unani medicine.def: Science by which one learns various states of human body, in health and when not in health, and means by which health is likely to be lost, and when lost, is likely to be restored (science of medicine/ Unani System of Me

theoretical aspect of medicine.def: Aspect of medicine concerned with theoretical topics such as temperaments, humours, faculties, various diseases, symptoms and causes

practical aspect of medicine.def: Aspect of medicine concerned with knowledge of medical management and practice, e.g., knowledge of methods of preserving health under various conditions and treating every kind of disease

subject matter of medicine.def: Subject of medicine, which is the human body in health and disease and as a whole deals with primary components/elements, humours, temperaments, simple and compound organs, pneuma, faculties and their functions; states o

material causes/ physical causes.def: One of four groups of causes of health and disease whose immediate subject is organs and their pneuma; the more remote subject is humours; and the most remote subject is the primary components / elements

acting causes/ efficient causes.def: One of four groups of causes of health and disease which either preserve or produce changes in the human body and are: (a) various types of airs and associated factors; (b) food, water and other drinks, etc.; (c) evacuat

formal causes / causes related to form.def: One of four groups of causes of health and disease which include temperaments, facul- ties emerging from these temperaments, and structures

logic.def: Reasonable thinking; tool to discover the unknown from the known, which saves the human being from errors in thinking

Nutq, An Arabic word, derived from which means to talk or speak. Mantiq Technically, or logic is a science through which the human mind is saved from making errors of thoughts or judgments. Its possible English equivalen

process of signifying by signs.def: The process of knowing one reality on the basis of reflection or expression of another factor or knowledge of another reality, leading to conclusive facts, e.g., yellowness of the face of a person denotes fear, where one

literal expression.def: Use of a statement in the complete meaning of the subject of declaration, e.g., when a person says that he saw the sun, he means to say that he saw the complete disc of the sun

figurative expression.def: Use of a statement in part of the meaning of the subject of the declaration, e.g., when one says that he saw the sun intending that he saw part of the disc of the sun at sunset time or intending that he saw the sunlight

universal concept.def: A statement about an object or phenomenon which has many members and is applied to all of them, e.g., human, which has many members and the statement human includes all of them

species.def: Group of members similar in essence; a type of universal concept, which covers all of its members who are similar in their essence, e.g., human, whose members are Zaid, Mohan, Jacob, etc.; they may differ in height, colo
A whole, referred to all individuals or varieties that are basically same for e.g. humans e.g. Zayd, Bakar, all belong to the same specie. Its possible English equivalent is species.

(1) members or units of a class having c.def: (1) A type of universal concept where members or units of a class vary in their reality and the variation is the part of reality of members, e.g., animal whose members are animals along with humans;they differ in their r

(1) distinguishing character of a group;.def: (1) A type of universal concept having members similar in reality and this similarity is part of the reality of its members, e.g., being rational is part of the reality of human and members of humanity are similar in rea

particular incidence/ particular occurre.def: A type of universal concept that is special for one reality and is outside of its main essence, e.g., laughing which is special for human but is not part of its reality and essence Description

common incidence/ common occurrence.def: A type of universal concept that is not special for one reality and is outside of its main essence, e.g., walking which includes realities other than human

partial.def: Statement having one meaning, which is applied to only a particular member
equivalent is absolute. A term that refers to a particular person or object (not shared by others) e.g. the name of a person. Its possible English equivalent is partial.

affirmation.def: Affirmation that helps to ascertain unknown facts

deduction of the state of universal conc.def: Reaching a conclusion regarding the state of universal concept from the state of divisive concepts; the conclusion will have specific feature in its universal concept, e.g., conclusion regarding the growth of the human b

syllogistic inference.def: Deduction of the state of divisive concept from a state of universal concept, i.e., reaching a conclusion regarding the state of divisive concept from the state of universal concept, e.g., concluding that John, a divisiv

deduction of the state of other divisive.def: Reaching a conclusion regarding the state of the divisive concept through the state of other divisive concepts, e.g., concluding that cannabis is illegal due to its similarity with liquor which is also illegal Descriptio

opined statement.def: Statement expressing an opinion; may be true or false and may be confirmed or rejected

contradictory proposition.def: Difference in two propositions where the truth of one of the two requires the falsehood of the other

philosophy.def: Reasoning to understand sensible objects; science which deals with sensible objects through applying logical reasoning
The study of all worldly objects living or non-living within the reach of human intellect or imagination is called Falsafa. Its possible English equivalent is philosophy.

(1) distinctive attribute; (2) condition.def: (1) Thing that does not exist on its own and depends on other things for its existence, e.g., colour; (2) An abnormal condition which appears due to any other disease, e.g., headache occurring due to fever

(1) thing existing on its own; (2) activ.def: (1) Thing that exists on its own and does not depend on any other thing for its existence, e.g., body; (2) Part of the drug obtained through specific processing methods which is comparatively more active than its other p

substance/material acquiring various for.def: Substance/material which acquires various forms and cannot exhibit itself without them

body(ies).def: Divisible thing(s); things divisible into length, width and depth

prime matter standard terminologies on u.def: Matter that can take any shape or form Description

visible shape.def: The determinate structure which gives things their essential characteristics

physical form.def: Form of a body which accepts the length, width and depth
zodiac. The physical outline of a body which gives it a certain shape and form. Its possible English equivalent is physical form.

factors of existence.def: Seven factors considered to be essential for the composition of the human body, which are responsible for its existence, i.e., primary components/elements, temperament, humours, organs, pneuma, faculties and functions
The four basic causes of a being to come into existence. Its possible English equivalent is factors of existence.

primary components / elements.unani: Al-Arkān / Ajsām Basīṭa/ Ajzā’ Awwaliyya/ Usṭuqussāt / ‘Anāṣir
primary components / elements.def: Simple bodies that cannot be subdivided into bodies having different forms and are primary substances of human and non-human bodies, producing different forms in nature through their intermixture. (“Al-Arkān” has also be

four primary components / elements.def: Primary components of human and non-human bodies, i.e., fire, air, earth and water, producing different forms in nature through their intermixture

earth standard terminologies on unani me.def: One of the four primary components /elements of human and non-human bodies having cold and dry quality, which remains situated at the centre of all existence, make the objects firm and stable, and maintain their forms an

temperament.def: Uniform quality of a compound present in its all particles in equal proportion, developed due to the interaction of opposite qualities of the four primary components in such a manner that most of the particles of each of

real equable temperament.def: Temperament wherein the opposite qualities and quantities of all the participating primary components/ elements in a compound are equal Description

equable temperament.unani: Mizāj Mu‘tadil Ṭibbī / Mizāj Mu‘tadil/‘Adl fi’l Qisma
equable temperament.def: Temperament wherein the qualities and quantities of all the participating primary components in a compound are not equal but perfectly balanced as per the required properties and functions of that compound

equable temperament of one species as co.unani: Mizāj Mu‘tadil Naw‘ī bi’l Qiyās ila’l Khārij
equable temperament of one species as co.def: Particular temperament provided to a particular species which is normal and most suitable for that species but abnormal for other species

equable temperament of a member of one s.unani: Mizāj Mu‘tadil Naw‘ī bi’l Qiyās ila’l Dākhil
equable temperament of a member of one s.def: Equable temperament provided to a member of a species which is most suitable and best among all the members of that species and has not been provided to any other member of that species

equable temperament of one race as compa.unani: Mizāj Mu‘tadil Ṣinfī bi’l Qiyās ila’l Khārij
equable temperament of one race as compa.def: Equable temperament provided to each race of human species, which helps to perform the required functions of each race with maximum completeness

equable temperament of a person of one r.unani: Mizāj Mu‘tadil Ṣinfī bi’l Qiyās ila’l Dākhil
equable temperament of a person of one r.def: Equable temperament provided to a person of a race which is not provided to any other person of that race and is better than all the persons of that race

equable temperament of a person as compa.unani: Mizāj Mu‘tadil Shakhṣī bi’l Qiyās ila’l Khārij
equable temperament of a person as compa.def: Equable temperament provided to a person which is most suitable for himself to perform his normal functions but is not suitable for any other person Description

equable temperament of a person as compa.unani: Mizāj Mu‘tadil Shakhṣī bi’l Qiyās ila’l Dākhil
equable temperament of a person as compa.def: Equable temperament provided to a person which is most suitable for a particular period or state in his life

equable temperament of an organ as compa.unani: Mizāj Mu‘tadil ‘Uḍwī bi’l Qiyās ila’l Khārij
equable temperament of an organ as compa.def: Equable temperament furnished to each and every organ of the body

equable temperament of an organ as compa.unani: Mizāj Mu‘tadil ‘Uḍwī bi’l Qiyās ila’l Dākhil
equable temperament of an organ as compa.def: Equable particular temperament provided to an organ in most suitable states which is most ideal as compared to all other states of that organ

abnormal temperament/ inequable temperam.def: Temperament wherein the qualities and quantities of all the participating primary components in a compound are not in accordance with the natural needs

simple abnormal temperament.def: Imbalanced temperament occurring due to dominance of any one of the four qualities, i.e., hotness, coldness, dryness, moistness, over the other three qualities

compound abnormal temperament.def: Imbalanced temperament occurring due to dominance of two of the four qualities, i.e., hotness, coldness, dryness, moistness
when any two of the four physical properties i.e. hot, cold, dry and moist dominates abnormally on the other. Its possible English equivalent is compound abnormal temperament.

humour(s) / body fluid(s).def: Primary fluid of the body which is an initial product of digestion and transformation of food

four humours.def: Four primary fluids of the body, i.e., sanguine, phlegm, yellow bile and black bile Description

sanguine.def: One of the four humours, which is characterized by hot and moist temperament, red colour, sweet taste and no odour; it is the best of all the four varieties of humours

normal sanguine.unani: Dam Ṭabī‘ī / Khūn Ṭabī‘ī/ Dam Maḥmūd
normal sanguine.def: Red-coloured, odourless and sweet in taste sanguine humour

abnormal sanguine.unani: Dam Ghayr Ṭabī‘ī / Ghayr Ṭabī‘ī Khūn
abnormal sanguine.def: Sanguine humour, devoid of its normal qualities either through change of temperament, such as getting hot or cold, or due to admixture with the humours derived from outside or produced within the sanguine humour, e.g., w

phlegm.def: One of the four humours, which is white in colour, bears cold and moist temperament and is next to sanguine humour in excellence
One of the four basic humours which is white in colour and bears cold and wet temperament. It is next to sanguine humour in excellence. Its possible English equivalent is phlegm.

normal phlegm.def: Phlegm that can be converted into sanguine humour by the body when needed

abnormal phlegm.def: Phlegm devoid of basic characters of normal phlegm

saline phlegm sour phlegm standard termi.unani: Balgham Māliḥ / Balgham Shor Balgham Ḥāmiḍ / Balgham Tursh
saline phlegm sour phlegm standard termi.def: One of the abnormal types of phlegm, which is saline or alkaline in taste and inclined towards hotness and dryness One of the abnormal types of phlegm, which is sour in taste and inclined towards coldness and dryness Des

tasteless phlegm.def: One of the abnormal types of phlegm, which is tasteless, very immature and quite cold

gallic phlegm; astringent phlegm.def: One of the abnormal types of phlegm, which is gallic in taste and inclined towards coldness and dryness

sweet phlegm.def: One of the abnormal types of phlegm, which is sweet in taste and inclined towards hotness and moistness
One of the abnormal types of phlegm which is sweet in taste. It is more inclined towards heat and moistness. Its possible English equivalent is sweet phlegm.

watery phlegm.def: One of the abnormal types of phlegm, having watery consistency

calcareous phlegm.def: One of the abnormal types of phlegm, having thicker consistency

mucoid phlegm.def: One of the abnormal types of phlegm, having mucoid consistency

vitreous phlegm.def: One of the abnormal types of phlegm, having vitreous consistency
An abnormal type of phlegm which has vitreous consistency. Its possible English equivalent is vitreous phlegm.

foul-smelling phlegm.def: One of the abnormal types of phlegm, having a foul smell, produced by sepsis/infection/ putrefaction

bile / yellow bile/ yellow humour/ bilio.def: One of the four humours, which is yellow in colour, has hot and dry temperament and is next to phlegm in excellence

normal yellow bile.def: Yellow bile that remains like the froth in sanguine humour, is yellowish red in colour, light in weight and hot in temperament Description

abnormal yellow bile.def: Yellow bile that becomes abnormal either due to addition of extraneous material or alteration in its own composition

vitelline yellow bile.def: One of the abnormal types of yellow bile, produced by the admixture of thick phlegm

serous yellow bile.def: One of the abnormal types of yellow bile, produced by the admixture of thin phlegm

burnt yellow bile.def: One of the abnormal types of yellow bile, produced by the admixture of burnt black bile

leek green yellow bile.def: One of the abnormal types of yellow bile, produced by the combustion of ordinary yellow bile, resulting in development of leek green colour due to mixing of the burnt material with the original yellow bile

verdigris green yellow bile.def: The hottest and the most harmful of all abnormal types of yellow bile, produced due to extreme combustion of leek green bile

black bile / black humour / melancholic.unani: Sawdā’ / Khilṭ Sawdā’/ Khilṭ Sawdāwī/ Mirra Sawdā’/ Al- Khilṭ al-Aswad
black bile / black humour / melancholic.def: One of the four humours, which is black in colour and has cold and dry temperament

normal black bile standard terminologies.def: One of the four humours, which is a sediment of normal sanguine with a taste between sweetness and astringency Description

abnormal black bile.def: Black bile produced either by combustion of black bile itself or combustion of any of the other three humours, i.e., sanguine, phlegm, yellow bile

sanguineous black bile.def: One of the abnormal types of black bile, produced by combustion of sanguine

bilious black bile.def: One of the abnormal types of black bile, produced by combustion of yellow bile

phlegmatic black bile.def: One of the abnormal types of black bile, produced by combustion of phlegm

melancholic black bile.def: One of the abnormal types of black bile, produced by combustion of black bile itself

organ(s).def: Parts of the body formed of normal humours that retain their own shapes

simple organs/ homogeneous organs.unani: A‘ḍā’ Mufrada / A‘ḍā’ Basīṭa / A‘ḍā’ Mutashābiha al-Ajzā’
simple organs/ homogeneous organs.def: Organs whose visible parts have the same name and definitions as the whole organ

bone(s).def: Hard and white simple organ forming skeleton

cartilage(s).def: Simple organ, which is softer than bone but relatively harder than other tissues

nerve(s).def: White and elastic simple organ originating from brain and the spinal cord Description

tendon(s).def: Simple organ bearing resemblance to nerve, which arises from the ends of muscle and gets attached to the mobile parts of the body

ligament(s).def: White simple organ bearing resemblance to nerve, strengthening the joint by joining one bone with another

artery(ies).def: Simple long hollow pulsating organ originating from heart, which contracts and expands with a period of rest in between the two movements

vein(s).def: Simple non-pulsating organ similar to artery, originating from liver

membrane(s).def: Simple organ made of invisible nerve-like fibres, which is spread into thin sheets over various organs

flesh.def: Simple organ that fills spaces between other simple organs

moist temperament. It forms bulk of the body and has many varieties. The most refined variety are the muscles and the most prominent function is body movements. Its possible English equivalent is flesh.

fat.def: White and soft simple organ having oily matter, which is commonly found over membranes

One of the simple organs having cold and wet temperament. After metabolism its temperament becomes hot and dry. It preserves the heat of the body and generates energy when needed. Its possible English equivalent is fat.

hair.def: Simple organ originating from thicker part of body vapours that keeps on accumulating into the skin pores and gets transformed into hair; it grows in length during whole lifetime of a person
skin. It is considered to be one of the simple organs having cold and dry temperament. Its possible English equivalent is hair.

nail(s) standard terminologies on unani.def: Simple organ which always grows in length during whole lifetime of a person and remains attached with distal phalanx Description

skin.def: Simple organ which forms outer covering of body
It is considered to be one of the simple organs having moderate temperament. Its possible English equivalent is skin.
The outer covering of the body mostly in association with skin. It works for one of the sensory powers i.e. power of taste. Its possible English equivalent is skin.

compound organs.def: Organs whose comprising parts, irrespective of size, differ in nature as well as name from the whole organ, e.g., hand, face, etc., where a part of the face cannot be called the face and a part of hand cannot be called t
The organs in which the comprising parts irrespective of size differ in nature as well as name for the whole organ. Its possible English equivalent is compound organs.

compound organs type i.def: Compound organs composed of only simple organs, e.g., muscle which is composed of flesh, fat, etc.

compound organs type ii.def: Compound organs composed of a compound organ type I with other organs, e.g., eye which is composed of muscle (a compound organ type I), fluids and seven layers

compound organs type iii.def: Compound organs composed of a compound organ type II with other organs, e.g., face which is composed of eye (a compound organ type II), nose, mouth, cheeks, etc.

compound organs type iv.def: Compound organs composed of a compound organ type III with other organs, e.g., head which is composed of face (a compound organ type III), ear, brain, etc.

organs for nutrition, growth and reprodu.def: Organs executing the functions of the physical faculty, with the liver being the chief organ

organs for nutrition and growth.def: Organs making suitable changes in the diet consumed by an individual, to make it part of his body and replace wear and tear Description

reproductive organs.def: Organs executing functions of reproductive faculty for preservation of species, the testes being the chief organ in males and the ovaries in females
possible English equivalent is reproductive organs. A‘da’ Tanasuliyya. Same as

organs for blood circulation and respira.def: Organs executing functions of vital faculty, the heart being the chief organ

organs for psychic functions.def: Organs executing the functions of the psychic faculty, the brain being the chief organ

pneuma(s).def: Light gaseous substance obtained from the interaction of inspired air with subtle humours found in organs and fluids of the body and help faculties in their functions; the term does not cover Rūḥ mentioned under Unani ph

psychic pneuma.def: The pneuma found in the organs of the psychic faculty whose centre is brain and helps psychic faculty to perform its functions
Ruh The (pneuma) which is found in the organs of psychic faculty and its centre is brain. Its possible English equivalent is psychic pneuma.

vital pneuma.def: The pneuma found in the organs of the vital faculty whose centre is heart and helps vital faculty to perform its functions
Ruh The (pneuma) which is found in the organs of vital faculty and its centre is heart. Its possible English equivalent is vital pneuma.

physical pneuma.def: The pneuma found in the organs of the physical faculty whose centre is liver and helps physical faculty to perform its functions

faculty(ies)/power(s) standard terminolo.def: One of the seven factors of existence, which provides the basis for different bodily functions Description

physical faculty.def: Faculty serving the functions of nutrition, growth, reproduction and evacuation of waste products from the body for the preservation of the individual as well as the species

primary physical faculties.def: Physical faculties responsible for preservation of the individual as well as the species

personal faculty.def: Primary physical faculty making alteration in the food for the preservation of the individual

nutritive faculty.def: One of the type of physical faculties making alteration in the food in such a manner that it becomes temperamentally similar to the body and suitable to replace daily wear and tear
The power which is specific for the digestion of food. Its possible English equivalent is nutritive faculty.

collecting faculty.def: One of the three components of nutritive faculty, which selects and collects the matter for digestion

adhesive faculty.def: One of the three components of the nutritive faculty, which attaches digested matter to the organs for their nutrition

faculty of assimilation.def: One of the three components of the nutritive faculty, which transforms the matter attached to the organ by the adhesive faculty in such a way that it resembles the nourished organ in all respects and becomes a part of th

faculty of growth.def: One of the physical faculties, which develops the organs in the required form and size and integrates the nutrient material to complete individual development Description

reproductive faculties.def: Primary physical faculties provided to an individual for preservation of its species

generative faculty.def: One of the reproductive faculties, which forms the male and female reproductive units

One of the reproductive faculties which isolate the generative matter from the basic substance of the body and transforms it into the reproductive matter i.e. sperm and ovum. Its possible English equivalent is generative

primary transformative faculty.def: Faculty in the male element separating various faculties in the sperm and rearranging them to provide each member the temperament appropriate for it

formative faculty.def: One of the reproductive faculties, which gives shape to each organ of body in accordance with the species or race
One of the reproductive faculties which give images to each organ of the body in resemblance to the species or race. Its possible English equivalent is formative faculty.

secondary physical faculties.def: Faculties that help in the actions of nutritive faculty

absorptive faculty.def: Faculty which serves the nutritive faculty and absorbs beneficial material into the body
nutritive faculty. One of the sub serving powers of Quwwat Ghadhiya. Its function is the absorption of food. Its possible English equivalent is absorptive faculty.

retentive faculty.def: Faculty which serves the nutritive faculty and retains the dietary material until the digestive faculty completes its work
One of the sub serving powers of Quwwat Ghadhiya. Its function is the retention of matter in the organs for their digestion. Its possible English equivalent is retentive faculty.

digestive faculty.def: Faculty which serves the nutritive faculty and digests the dietary material to make it part of an organ

expulsive faculty vital faculty standard.def: Faculty which serves the nutritive faculty and expels waste products from body Faculty which is essential for life and reaches from heart to body organs through arteries and keeps them alive Description

psychic faculty / mental faculty.def: Faculty which is furnished in an individual for sensory/perceptive and motor/motive function of the body; it controls nervous tissues to perform the functions of sensation/perception and regulates the nervous system for

motor faculty/ motor power.def: Type of psychic and mental faculty which regulates motor activities in the body; the motor faculty contracts and relaxes the tendons, through which the organs and the joints extend and flex

motivating faculty/ preparatory faculty.unani: Quwwat Shawqiyya / Quwwat Nuzū‘iyya/ Quwwat Isti‘dādiyya
motivating faculty/ preparatory faculty.def: One of the motor faculties that prepares the mind to do something either in favour or against the will

desiring faculty.def: A subserving faculty of the motivating faculty, which directs the mind to obtain things perceived to be useful

raging power/ protective faculty.def: A subserving faculty of the motivating faculty, which directs the mind for a reaction against a threat or an event comprehended to be harmful

stimulating power/ efficient faculty.def: One of the motor faculties, which helps in directing the organs for actions

receptive faculty/ sensory faculty/ perc.def: Type of psychic faculty which regulates sensory/ perceptive functions of body

external receptive faculty.def: One of the sensory faculties, which help to interpret the outer sensations of the body Description

five external senses.def: Five external senses, executed by external sense organs, i.e., eye, ear, nose, tongue and skin

power/sense of vision.def: Capability of eyes to focus and detect images/ power which performs the functions of vision

hearing power.def: Power/sense which perceives sounds and performs the function of hearing

power of taste.def: One of the sub serving powers of motor faculty. Its function is the perception of taste. Its possible English equivalent is power of taste.

tactile sensation.def: Power which perceives sensation of touch

internal perceptive faculty/ receptive p.def: One of the sensory faculties, which recognises, receives and collects the sensations forwarded by external senses; this faculty has its centre/ seat inside the brain

faculty of composite sense.def: One of the five internal perceptive faculties, which receives all sensations, composes them into percepts and enables proper sensory appreciations

faculty of imagination standard terminol.def: One of the five internal perceptive faculties, which preserves knowledge perceived by the faculty of composite sense and the knowledge or sense with which new sensory experiences of the same or similar types can be compa

faculty of imagination with interpretati.def: One of the five internal perceptive faculties, which interprets the meanings of those particular forms perceived by faculty of composite sense and decides what is in favour of an individual and what is against him or her

faculty of memory / cognitive faculty/ m.def: One of the five internal perceptive faculties, which preserves the meanings derived by the faculty of imagination with interpretation; the memory may be instantaneous, short-term or long-term

faculty of modification/ administration.unani: Quwwat Mutaṣarrifa / Quwwat Mufakkira/ Quwwat Mutakhayyila
faculty of modification/ administration.def: One of the five internal perceptive faculties, which modifies various sensory information in various ways and gives new dimensions to preserved knowledge

function(s).def: Bodily activity, essential to fulfil the objectives of the body, which is performed by the organs and is testimony to the presence of faculties in them

physical functions.def: Functions which are essential to energize all powers and develop the organs

function of nutrition.def: Function performed by the digestive faculty which digests and transforms food to become the part of the body

function of absorption.def: Process of absorption of food controlled by the absorptive faculty

function of retention.def: Function of retaining food at all sites of digestion, controlled by the retentive faculty Description

function of digestion.def: Function of digestion and transformation of food into nutriment, controlled by the digestive faculty

function of expulsion.def: Function of expulsion of undigested and waste matter from the body, controlled by the expulsive faculty

function of growth.def: Function of nourishment and growth of the body, controlled by the physical faculty

function of reproduction.def: Function of reproduction, controlled by the reproductive faculty for continuation of the species

psychic functions.def: Functions controlled by the psychic faculty
The functions that are performed by the psychic faculty of the body. These are Quwa Nafsaniyya performed by and executed A‘da’ Nafsaniyya. by Its possible English equivalent is psychic functions.

sensory function.def: Function controlled by the sensory faculty through sensory nerves and associated organs
The function which is performed by sensory power and is executed by the sensory nerves and the associated organs. Its possible English equivalent is sensory function.

motor function.def: The function which is controlled by the motor faculty and is executed by motor nerves and associated organs; this term does not cover Ḥarakat, mentioned under general terms; this is in the context of function of motor ne
The function which is performed by motor power and is executed by motor nerves and their associated organs. Its possible English equivalent is motor function.

function of intellect standard terminolo.def: Function controlled by the intellectual faculty; it depends on the level of intellect of an individual and thereby differs from person to person; this term does not cover Tadbīr, mentioned under Unani pharmacy and therap

imagination.def: Function performed when intellectual faculties, such as faculty of modification, give new dimensions to perceived sensations

consideration/ contemplation/act of thin.def: Function performed when intellectual faculties, such as the faculty of imagination with interpretation, establish the meaning of the perceived sensations

memorization.def: Function performed when intellectual faculties, such as faculty of memory, preserve knowledge forwarded by the faculty of imagination with interpretation

desire.def: Function of mind awakening to fulfil the needs of the body under the influence of the desiring faculty

vital functions.def: Functions which are indispensable for survival and are performed by the vital faculties through the vital organs
Quwa survival. These are performed by Haywaniyya A‘da’ and executed by Haywaniyya. Its possible English equivalent is vital functions.

simple function.def: Function or act performed by only one power under any of the faculties, e.g., act of vision, act of olfaction
A simple function or act which is performed by only one power under any of the faculties e.g. acts of vision, act of olfaction etc. Its possible English equivalent is simple function.

compound function.def: Function or act performed by more than one power or faculty, e.g.,digestion, respiration
A compound function or act which is performed by more than one power or faculty e.g. digestion, respiration etc. Its possible English equivalent is compound function.

medicatrix naturae.unani: Ṭabī‘at / Al-Ṭabī‘a al-Insāniyya/ Al-Ṭabī‘a al-Mudabbira li’l Badan
medicatrix naturae.def: Natural power for self-preservation; the power endowed by nature to every individual for self-preservation; it regulates normal functions and is the administrator, protector and healer of the body Description

factor maintaining medicatrix naturae.def: Factor that comprises the structure and temperament of an organ or the whole body and is necessary for maintenance of medicatrix naturae

single primary component/element.def: A group of ancient philosophers held the view that everything in the world was made of only water, earth, fire or air

multiple primary components/elements.def: A group of philosophers put forth the concept of an unlimited number of elements and said that primary elements of all things in the universe were plentiful and could not be counted

fiery primary components/elements.def: Primary components/elements bearing the natural properties of hotness and dryness and performing activities like fire

airy primary components/elements.def: Primary components/elements bearing the natural properties of hotness and moistness and performing activities like air

watery primary components/elements.def: Primary components/elements bearing the natural properties of coldness and moistness and performing activities like water

earthy primary components/elements.def: Primary components/elements bearing the natural properties of coldness and dryness and performing activities like earth

physical properties/ physical qualities/.def: Four physical properties or qualities i.e., hotness, coldness, moistness/wetness and dryness, associated with matter

active properties standard terminologies.def: Two active properties, i.e.,hotness and coldness Description

hotness.def: One of the two active properties naturally associated with matter

coldness.def: One of the two active properties naturally associated with matter

passive properties.def: Two passive physical properties, i.e., moistness/ wetness and dryness

moistness/wetness.def: One of the two passive physical properties naturally associated with matter

dryness.def: One of the two passive physical properties naturally associated with matter
One of the four physical properties naturally associated with matter. Its possible English equivalent is dryness. Simple bodies which are primary

innate heat / natural heat.def: Heat of the body regulated by medicatrix naturae to maintain life

morbid heat / abnormal heat.def: Abnormal increase of heat disturbing body functions

hypothermia / insufficient heat.def: Abnormal deficit of body heat disturbing normal bodily functioning

admixture/intermixture.def: Intermixture of primary components/elements resulting in variable outcomes, depending on their individual properties

simple intermixture/ simple mixture.def: A type of intermixture in which two or more primary components/elements are mixed in a manner allowing their previous properties to remain unchanged Description

complex intermixture.def: A type of intermixture in which two or more primary components/elements are mixed in a manner causing their previous properties to be changed

chemical affinity.def: Property of having chemical attraction present in some primary components/elements or compounds, which enables them to combine with certain other primary components/elements or compounds

constitution. The property of having chemical attraction in some elements or compounds which enables them to combine with certain other elements or compounds. Its possible English equivalent is chemical affinity.

chemical repulsion.def: Property of having chemical repulsion present in some primary components or components/ elements or compounds, which enables them to repel from combining with other primary components or compounds

ten identifying features of temperament.def: Ten features including complexion of the body, build, texture of the skin, condition of hair, movement of body, diet, quantum of sleep, seasons in which the person is most healthy and least healthy, character of pulse, e

primary temperament / basic temperament.unani: Mizāj Awwalī / M izāj Khilqī/ Mizāj Jibillī
primary temperament / basic temperament.def: Temperament inherited by the progeny which prevails throughout the life as identity and is not likely to change

secondary temperament/ acquired temperam.def: Temperament developed in an individual under the influence of efficient causes like climate, food,drinks, etc. Description

human temperament.def: The temperament of human beings, which is considered to be the most perfect and noble in comparison with all other species and is inclined towards heat

The temperament of human beings which is considered to be the most perfect and noble in comparison with all other species. This temperament is inclined towards heat. Its possible English equivalent is human temperament.

hot and dry temperament.def: Temperament in which the hot and dry qualities dominate the other two qualities, i.e., cold and moist
physical properties dominate the other two physical properties i.e. cold and moist. Its possible English equivalent is hot and dry temperament.

hot and moist temperament.def: Temperament in which the hot and moist qualities dominate the other two qualities, i.e., cold and dry
The temperament in which the hot and moist physical properties dominate the other two physical properties i.e. cold and dry. Its possible English equivalent is hot and moist temperament.

cold and dry temperament.def: Temperament in which the cold and dry qualities dominate the other two qualities, i.e., hot and moist
The temperament in which the cold and dry physical properties dominate the other two physical properties i.e. hot and moist. Its possible English equivalent is cold and dry temperament.

cold and moist temperament.def: Temperament in which the cold and moist properties dominate the other two qualities, i.e., hot and dry
The temperament in which the cold and moist properties dominate on the other two physical properties i.e. hot and dry. Its possible English equivalent is cold and moist temperament.

sanguineous temperament.def: A type of temperament caused by the predominance of Dam (sanguine) in the body, which is hot and moist; individuals with this type of temperament have strong build, full pulse, pinkish colour of skin, etc.

A type of temperament caused by the predominance of Dam (sanguine) in the body. It is hot and moist. The individuals with this type of temperament have strong built, full pulse, pinkish colour of skin etc. Its possible E

phlegmatic temperament.def: A type of temperament caused by the predominance of Balgham (phlegm) in the body, which is cold and moist; individuals with this type of temperament have fatty body, excessive sleep disposition, whitish colour of skin, e

A type of temperament caused by the predominance of Balgham (phlegm) in the body. It is cold and moist. The individuals with this type of temperament have fatty body, excessive sleep and whitish colour of skin etc. Its p

bilious temperament.def: A type of temperament caused by the predominance of Ṣafrā’ (yellow bile) in the body, which is hot and dry; individuals with this type of temperament have thin build, yellowish colour of the skin, rapid pulse, etc.

A type of temperament caused by the Safra’ predominance of (yellow bile) in the body. It is hot and dry. The individuals with this type of temperament have thin built, yellowish colour of the skin, rapid pulse etc. Its p

melancholic temperament.def: A type of temperament caused by the predominance of Sawdā’ (black bile) in the body, which is cold and dry; individuals with this type of temperament have thin build with prominent veins, blackish skin, etc.

A type of temperament caused by the Sawda’ predominance of (black bile) in the body. It is cold and dry. The individuals with this type of temperament have thin built with prominent veins, blackish color of the skin etc.

temperament of gender.def: Term used to explain the difference between the temperament of masculine and feminine gender

feminine temperament.def: Temperament of females, i.e., colder and more moist as compared with males
The temperament of females i.e. cold and moist in comparison with males. Its possible English equivalent is feminine temperament.

masculine temperament.def: Temperament of males, i.e., hot and less moist in comparison with females
The temperament of males i.e. hot and less moist in comparison with females. Its possible English equivalent is masculine temperament.

four stages of life.def: Four stages of the entire period of life, i.e., growing age, adulthood, age of decline and age of elderly

growing age.def: Age in which the body continues to grow, has hot and moist temperament, extending from birth to thirty years

infancy childhood standard terminologies.def: The age of growth extending from birth to four years of age The age of growth extending from four to nine years Description

adolescence.def: The age of growth extending from nine to fourteen years
Sinn-i-Numu The age which comes under and extends from nine to fourteen years of age. Its possible English equivalent is adolescence.

puberty.def: The time in life when a boy or girl becomes sexually mature

final stage of growing age.def: Stage of growing age extending from twenty-five to thirty years

adulthood.def: The age in which the body becomes fully mature and stable and has the most appropriate moderate hot and moist temperament; it extends from thirty to forty years

age of decline.def: The age when innate heat starts to decline, the body inclines to adopt cold and dry temperament, loses its stability and starts to deteriorate; it extends from forty to sixty years

age of elderly / geriatric age.def: The age when innate heat has declined, the body adopts cold and dry temperament and there is excess of abnormal moisture in the body; it extends from sixty years onwards

temperament of different age groups.def: Temperament of an individual in different phases of his/her life

temperament of children.def: Temperament which remains hot and moist during childhood

temperament of adults.def: Temperament which remains hot and moist but the moistness is comparatively less than in the period of childhood Description

temperament of the age of decline.def: Temperament which ordinarily remains cold and dry during the age of decline

temperament of elderly/senile.def: Temperament which remains cold and dry along with dominance of abnormal moistness

temperament of occupation.def: Adaptive changes in temperament of human beings under the influence of different occupations

inhabited quadrant.def: The quadrant of the earth which was supposed to be inhabited by ancient philosophers
The philosophers maintained that only one quadrant of the earth was inhabited and that quadrant is known as Rub‘ Maskun. Its possible English equivalent is inhabited quadrant.

seven regions / seven zones.def: Seven equal belts from the equator to the north pole into which the earth has been divided; each has its own temperament

moderate zone.unani: Iqlīm Mu‘tadil / Mamālik Mu‘tadila/ Buldān Mu‘tadila
moderate zone.def: Region or belt of the earth which is considered to be the most suitable habitat for human beings; Avicenna considered the first belt or the tropical zone and Fakhruddin Razi considered the fourth belt to be the moderate
possible English equivalent is moderate zone. Iqlim Same as Mu‘tadil.

regional temperament / habitable tempera.def: Temperament based on climatic conditions of a region; it is accredited as being suitable or otherwise for its inhabitants

humoural theory standard terminologies o.def: The body contains four humours, Dam (sanguine), Balgham (phlegm), Ṣafrā’ (yellow bile) and Sawdā’ (black bile); these are essential for the maintenance of health, and the overall effect or the combined qualities of these

production of humours.def: The process involved in the production of humours in the body

digestion.def: Breaking down of food particles with the help of innate heat of the body, followed by assimilation
down. It is actually the breaking down of the food particles with the help of innate heat of the body followed by its assimilation. Its possible English equivalent is digestion.

four stages of digestion.def: Four different stages of digestion through which food particles are assimilated in the body

alimentary digestion/ primary digestion.unani: Haḍm Mi‘dī / Haḍm Awwal/ Taghayyur Mi‘dī/ Haḍm Kaymūsī
alimentary digestion/ primary digestion.def: First stage of food digestion, occurring in the alimentary canal to form chyme

hepatic digestion / secondary digestion.unani: Haḍm Kabidī / Haḍm Dom/Taghayyur Kabidī / Haḍm Kaylūsī
hepatic digestion / secondary digestion.def: Second stage of food digestion taking place in the liver to form chyle

vascular digestion/ tertiary digestion.def: Third stage of digestion taking place in the vessels and helping in assimilation of food to make it part of the organs

organic digestion / fourth digestion / t.def: Fourth stage of digestion which takes place in the organs

metabolism.def: Transformation of food to become part of the body
management. The process of bio-chemical changes taking place in the body. Its possible English equivalent is metabolism.

replacement of fluids and energy.def: Process of replacement of fluids and energy utilized during the performance of regular functions, growth and repair of the body

seats of humours.def: Places where humours stay in the body, mainly the blood vessels Description

primitive fluid.def: Fluid transferred by parents to newborns through their germ cells or the fluid that exists in the tissue elements since birth

innate humour.def: The moist part of an organ, essential to bind it together; when this moisture is lost the particles of the organ disintegrates quickly
in equilibrium, qualitatively and quantitatively, in accordance to the temperament of that organ. Its possible English equivalent is innate humour.

primary fluid.def: Fluid found inside the blood vessels which contains all the four humours

The fluids found inside the blood vessels containing all four humours: sanguineous, bilious, phlegmatic and melancholic humours. Its possible English equivalent is primary fluid. The fluids which are derived from

secondary fluid.def: Fluid derived from primary fluids, which has changed from its initial state and has penetrated through the organ but has not yet become a part of any organ; it is found outside the blood vessels

enclosed fluid/humour in minute vessels.def: Type of secondary fluid enclosed in the cavities of the extremities of minute vessels in proximity of the principal organs which they irrigate

interstitial fluid / dewy humour.def: A type of secondary fluid which permeates in the interiors of organs like dew drops and is capable of being transformed into nutrient whenever such a need arises

nearly congealed fluid.def: A type of secondary fluid which is about to congeal and become part of an organ

seminal fluid normal humour/ healthy hum.unani: Ruṭūbat Manawiyya Khilṭ Ṣāliḥ / K hilṭ Maḥmūd/ Khilṭ Jayyid/ Ḥasan al-Kaymūs
seminal fluid normal humour/ healthy hum.def: A type of secondary fluid responsible for reproduction The best kind of humour, which has the capacity of becoming part of an organ and provides nourishment Description

abnormal humour.unani: Khilṭ Radī / Khilṭ Fāsid/ Khilṭ Ghayr Maḥmūd
abnormal humour.def: The kind of humour which is incapable of becoming part of an organ

raw humour/ immature humour.def: A type of humour which is immature and not capable of either being part of the body or providing nourishment to it
not yet ready to become a part of the nourished organ, which means it is not fully metabolized. Its possible English equivalent is raw humour/ immature humour.

effused humour.def: A type of humour which flows from the organ that produces it towards another organ to induce an action
A type of humour which flows from its producing organ to the other organ for its action. Its possible English equivalent is effused humour.

superfluous humour.def: equivalent is colloidal humour. The humour which is produced in excess. Its possible English equivalent is superfluous humour.

colloidal humour.def: A kind of abnormal humour in which the consistency of the matter is not constant throughout and shows imperfect metabolism

subtle humours.def: Humours that diffuse easily in the organs

source of pneuma.def: Mixture of a part of inhaled air and subtle humours

essence of pneuma.def: Component of pneuma, which comes into existence after getting separated from inhaled air in the lungs and mixed with the blood

carrier of pneuma.def: Agent which carries the essence of inspired air to all parts of the body

seats of pneuma.def: Places from where the pneuma originates, e.g., heart, brain, liver

routes of pneuma.def: Routes through which pneuma disperses in the body Description

movement of pneuma.def: Movement of pneuma from one place to another in the body along with the humours

heat generation.def: The process of generation of heat in the body, which involves action and reaction among pneuma and subtle humours
The generation of heat to maintain the innate heat of the body. Its possible English equivalent is heat generation.

heat loss.def: The loss of body heat to maintain its normal scale
The loss of body heat to maintain its normal scale. Its possible English equivalent is heat loss.

dense humours.def: Humours that perform constructive functions, become part of the organs, replenish wear and tear and promote growth

essential organs.unani: A‘ḍā’ Aṣliyya / A‘ḍā’ Manawiyya/ A‘ḍā’ Awwaliyya
essential organs.def: Organs which come into existence by the contents of semen, and which, once damaged, cannot be regenerated

haematogenic organs/ secondary organs.def: Organs which are produced by the humours and can be regenerated

serving organ.def: Organ that serves the power/function to other organs, e.g.,the heart is a serving organ for the blood vessels

receiving organ.def: An organ that receives power from other organs, e.g., blood vessels receive blood from the heart and nerves receive commands/ interpretations from the brain

vital organs standard terminologies on u.def: The organs which are the source of primary faculties in the body and are indispensable for the existence of the individual or the species Description

master organ.def: The brain, which sustains all the other faculties; it controls most of the activities of the body

principal organs / served organs.def: Organs which are served by other organs for their functions

subservient organs.def: Organs which serve other organs in functioning of the body
The inferior organs which play secondary role in the functioning of the body. Its possible English equivalent is subservient organs.

pre-serving organ.def: The helping organ which acts before the principal organ in execution of a particular function

post-serving organ.def: The organ which serves after the act of the principal organ in accomplishing the needs of a function

nutritive organ.def: The organ which prepares the nutrients for other organs
The organ which prepares the nutrient for other organs. Its possible English equivalent is nutritive organ.

nourished organ.def: An organ which consumes nutrients for its function and growth
The organ which consumes the nutrient for its function and growth. Its possible English equivalent is nourished organ.

brain.def: One of the vital organs having cold and moist temperament; it is the seat of mental faculties, sensation and movement
One of the vital organs. It is the seat of mental faculties, sensation and movement. It has cold and moist temperament. Its possible English equivalent is brain.

heart.def: One of the vital organs having hot and wet temperament, which is the seat of the vital faculty
One of the vital organs. It is the seat of vital faculty or innate heat. It has hot and moist temperament. Its possible English equivalent is heart.

liver.def: One of the vital organs having hot and wet temperament, which is the seat of the nutritive and vegetative faculties Description

gonads/testicles.def: Sex glands that are components of the vital organs and serve as the seat of reproductive faculties

hard organs.def: Organs having cold and dry temperament and hard texture, the hardest being the tooth
The organs having cold and dry temperament and hard texture. The hardest one is the tooth. Its possible English equivalent is hard organs.

soft organs.def: Organs having cold and moist temperament and soft texture, the softest being the brain
The organs having cold and moist temperament and soft texture. The softest one is the brain. Its possible English equivalent is soft organs.

hot organs.def: Organs with hot temperament which are metabolically very active and rich in blood supply; the hot organs include heart, liver, etc.

English equivalent is moderate organs. The organs which are hot in nature. They are very active with high rate of metabolism and rich in blood supply. Heart is the hottest of all organs followed by liver, muscles, kidney

cold organs.def: Organs with cold temperament, which are metabolically less active and have a relatively weak blood supply; the cold organs include bone, cartilage, ligament, etc.

The organs which are cold in nature. They are less active with low rate of metabolism and least blood supply. Bone is the coldest of all organs followed by cartilage, tendons, ligaments etc. Its possible English equivale

wet/moist organs.def: Organs with wet/moist temperament, soft and with relatively high amount of water; the moist organs include fat, brain, spinal cord, glands, etc.

dry organs.def: Organs with dry temperament, very hard and with a relatively low amount of water; the dry organs include hair, teeth, bone, tendon, cartilage, etc.

The organs which are dry in nature. They are very hard with least amount of water. The most dry organ is hair followed by teeth, tendon, bones, cartilage etc. Its possible English equivalent is dry organs.

glandular organs weak organ.def: Organs with special character of secretion An organ that becomes weak due to disease/ unhealthy conditions

conditions for nutrition.def: Certain conditions regarding the quality and quantity of food which have to be fulfilled for the proper nutrition of the body

conditions for growth and development.def: Certain conditions regarding the temperament of an individual and the organs which have to be fulfilled for the proper growth and development of the body

the basic components of the body.def: Humours and fluids that take part in formation of the body; they carry all the characters of the species as well as the race

sperm.def: A mature male reproductive cell consisting of head, neck, body and tail. Its possible English equivalent is sperm.

homologous compound.def: A term that applies to compounds which physically appear as homologous but consist of different ingredients; Hippocrates used this term for semen, which was said to be apparently uniform but contained different particles

conception of pregnancy.def: The process of implantation of the fertilized ovum in the uterus

formation of fetus.def: The process of transformation of conceived material into fetus Description

sexual power.def: The power which creates attraction between male and female followed by sexual urge, thus enabling them to perform the sexual act

voluntary power / implementing power.def: Willpower / voluntary power for the implementation of thoughts; the faculty which helps in implementing thoughts established by the modifying faculty

intellectual faculty.def: The main powers of the brain which analyse information perceived by the sensory powers

individual with intellect.def: Individual with intellect having powers of perception, cognition and expression, etc; the unique power of perception, cognition and expression which has been endowed only in the human beings by nature, making them superi

human cognitive faculty/ intellectual po.def: The power of thinking or intellect which is exclusively found in human beings

psychic reactions structure of organs.def: Reactions produced as a result of factors affecting the psychic faculty, e.g., happiness, sorrow, anger, fear, etc.

anatomy/ structure of organs.def: Branch of science which deals with the study of structure, shape, size, location,relations, etc., of the human body

frontal bone.def: One of the flat bones situated anteriorly. Its possible English equivalent is frontal bone.

temporal bone.def: A skull bone situated one each on the two sides of the temples. Its possible English equivalent is temporal bone.

sphenoid bone.def: Bone that supports all the skull bones and makes the base of skull. Its possible English equivalent is sphenoid bone.

ethmoid bone.def: One of the skull bones, situated anteriorly which forms skeleton of the nose. Its possible English equivalent is ethmoid bone.

mandible.def: A strong bone in the face that forms the lower jaw. It also helps in mastication. Its possible English equivalent is mandible.

hyoid bone.def: A “U” shaped bone between the chin and thyroid cartilage that provides attachment to the floor of the mouth and tongue. Its possible English equivalent is hyoid bone.

cervical vertebrae.unani: Fiqār al-‘Unuq / Faqarāt al-‘Unuq / Kharaz al-‘Unuq

cervical vertebrae.def: column. The seven bones situated at the neck. Its possible English equivalent is cervical vertebrae.

false ribs / floating ribs.unani: Aḍlā‘ al-Zūr / Al-Aḍlā‘ al-Nāqiṣa/ Al- Aḍlā‘ al-Khalaf

clavicle.def: possible English equivalent is clavicle. A flat bone, which forms the anterior median part of the thorax. Its possible English equivalent is sternum.

scapula.def: A large, flat and triangular bone situated at the back sides of thorax. Its possible English equivalent is scapula.

humerus.def: A long bone of the upper arm between the elbows and shoulder joint. Its possible English equivalent is humerus.

radius.def: The lateral bone of the forearm. Its possible English equivalent is radius.

femur.def: The longest and strongest bone of the body situated between hip and knee joint. Its possible English equivalent is femur.

tibia.def: The bone of leg at medial side. Its possible English equivalent is tibia.

fibula.def: A smaller bone of leg situated at lateral side. Its possible English equivalent is fibula.

patella.def: The largest sesamoid bone round in shape, situated in front of knee. Its possible English equivalent is patella.

phalanges of foot.def: Bones found in the toe, smaller in size and fourteen in numbers in each foot, two for great toe and three for each of the toes. Its possible English equivalent is phalanges of foot.

shoulder joint.def: A joint formed by articulation of the scapula and the head of the humerus. Its possible English equivalent is shoulder joint.

elbow joint.def: A joint in which the capitulum of humerus articulates with upper surface of the head of the radius and trochlea of the humerus articulates with trochlear notch of the ulna. Its possible English equivalent is elbow joint.

wrist joint.def: possible English equivalent is wrist joint. The joints between the bones of the wrist. Its possible English equivalent is intercarpal joint.

hip joint.def: The joint is the articulation of head of the femur with the acetabulum of the hip bone. Its possible English equivalent is hip joint.

knee joint.def: The joint is formed by condyles of the femur and tibia and patella. It is a compound synovial joint. Its possible English equivalent is knee joint.

ankle joint.def: The joint is formed by the lower end of tibia, lateral malleolus of the fibula and talus. Its possible English equivalent is ankle joint.

temporalis.def: A muscle located on the temporal part of the head. It is supplied by deep temporal branch of mandibular nerve. Its possible English equivalent is temporalis.

muscle which bends head and neck togethe.unani: Al-‘Aḍala al-Munakkisa li’l Ra’s Ma‘ al-Raqaba

muscles which turn the head and neck bac.unani: Al-‘Aḍal al-Muqalliba li’l Ra’s Ma‘ al-‘Unuq

muscles which perform the action of late.unani: Al-‘Aḍal al-Mumīla li’l Ra’s ila’l Jānibayn

muscles of tongue.def: A group of muscles making up the tongue

pronator muscles of forearm extensor mus.unani: Al-‘Aḍal al-Mukibba li’l Sā‘id Al-‘Aḍal al-Bāsiṭa li’l Rusgh

muscles which bend the spine backward.unani: Al-‘Aḍal al-Thāniya li’l Ṣulb ilā Khalf

adductor magnus muscle abductor muscles.unani: ‘Aḍala Insiyya li’l Fakhidh Al-‘Aḍal al-Mub‘ida li’l Fakhidh

cranial nerves.def: The nerves arising directly from brain. These are 12 pairs. Its possible English equivalent is cranial nerves.

sensory nerves.def: Nerves that carry senses. Its possible English equivalent is sensory nerves.

motor nerves.def: Nerves carrying orders from brain to the organs. Its possible English equivalent is motor nerves.

cervical nerves.def: Eight nerves originating from cervical vertebrae. Its possible English equivalent is cervical nerves.

thoracic nerves.def: The nerves originating from the thoracic vertebrae, 12 in number. Its possible English equivalent is thoracic nerves.

lumbar nerves.def: The nerves originating from the lumbar vertebrae, 5 in number. Its possible English equivalent is lumbar nerves.

sacral nerves.def: The nerves originating from the sacral vertebrae, 5 in number. Its possible English equivalent is sacral nerves.

coccygeal nerve.def: The nerve that originates from the coccyx. Its possible English equivalent is coccygeal nerve.

aorta.def: The largest artery which arises from the left ventricle of the heart. Its possible English equivalent is aorta.

ascending aorta.def: Part of aorta which begins at the base of ventricle. Its possible English equivalent is ascending aorta.

descending aorta superior vena cava stan.unani: Al-Shiryān al-Nāzil Ajwaf Ṣā‘id / Ajwaf A‘lā

inferior vena cava.def: A large venous channel which collects the blood from the lower half of the body and terminates by opening in the right atrium. Its possible English equivalent is inferior vena cava.

splenic vein.def: Vein formed at the hilum of the spleen, joins the superior mesenteric vein to form the portal vein. Its possible English equivalent is splenic vein.

axillary vein.def: A large vein of upper limb that accompanies axillary artery. Its possible English equivalent is axillary vein.

internal jugular vein.def: The main vein of head and neck which drains into the subclavian vein. Its possible English equivalent is internal jugular vein.

cephalic vein.def: The preaxial vein of upper limb. Its possible English equivalent is cephalic vein.

median cubital vein.def: cephalic vein and is often used for venesection. It lies in the cubital fossa. Its possible English equivalent is median cubital vein.

renal vein.def: Veins that collect blood from the capillary network of the renal cortex. It opens in the inferior vena cava slightly above the right vein. Its possible English equivalent is renal vein. Veins that follow the homonymous

popliteal vein.def: One of the deep veins of leg, formed by union of the branches of the anterior and posterior tibial veins. At the adductor hiatus it becomes continuous with the femoral vein. Its possible English equivalent is popliteal v

thyroid cartilage.def: The largest cartilages of larynx. Its possible English equivalent is thyroid cartilage.

arytenoid cartilage.def: The cartilage of the larynx is located at the postero superior border of the cricoid cartilage. Its possible English equivalent is arytenoid cartilage.

pancreas.def: A tongue shaped organ, having a head, tail and the body. One of its important functions is the secretion of insulin. Its possible English equivalent is pancreas.

uvula.def: equivalent is pharynx. A conical projection situated in the middle of inferior border of soft palate. Its possible English equivalent is uvula.

tonsils.def: Two almond shaped structures situated in the oral part of the pharynx. Its possible English equivalent is tonsils.

oesophagus.def: A narrow muscular tube, connecting the pharynx with the stomach. Its possible English equivalent is oesophagus.

peritoneum.def: A serous membrane lining the abdominal cavity and covering the viscera. This term is common to aponeuorosis and peritoneum. Its possible English equivalent is peritoneum.

small intestine.def: The thin intestines which are divided into three parts, namely duodenum, jejunum and ileum. Its possible English equivalent is small intestine.

duodenum.def: A part of small intestine that extends from duodeno-jejunal flexure. the pylorus to the Its possible English equivalent is duodenum.

jejunum.def: It constitutes the upper two fifths of the mobile part of the small intestine. Its possible English equivalent is jejunum.

ileum.def: It constitutes the lower three fifths of the mobile part of the small intestine. Its possible English equivalent is ileum.

large intestine.def: The part of intestines that extends from the small intestine up to the anus. Its possible English equivalent is large intestine.

caecum.def: One of the three parts of the large intestine and lies between the small intestine and the colon. Its possible English equivalent is caecum.

colon.def: The part of the large intestine extending from the caecum up to the rectum. Its possible English equivalent is colon.

rectum.def: The distal part of the large intestine. It is situated between the sigmoid colon above and the anal canal below. Its possible English equivalent is rectum.

ureters.def: A pair of narrow, thick walled muscular tubes which carry urine from the kidneys to the urinary bladder. Its possible English equivalent is ureters.

urinary bladder.def: A muscular reservoir of urine which lies in the anterior part of the pelvic cavity. Its possible English equivalent is urinary bladder.

cervix.def: The lower, cylindrical and narrow part of the uterus. Its possible English equivalent is cervix.

functions of body organs.def: Subject dealing with normal functions of organs or body

functions of sanguine.def: One of the four body humours whose function is to provide nutrition to the body organs, growth of tissues and organs, production of heat, regulation of body temperature and maintenance of colour of the skin

functions of yellow bile.def: One of the four body humours which liquefies the blood so that it can pass through narrow passages, contributes to the formation of specific organs, takes part in the metabolism of certain nutrients, removes viscous phle

functions of phlegm.def: One of the four body humours which gets transformed into blood whenever needed, keeps tissues and organs moist to avoid dryness caused by constant friction, maintains fluidity and viscosity of blood and works as the main

functions of black bile.def: One of the four body humours which maintains viscosity of blood, takes part in nutrition of specific organs, provides hardness to some organs and stimulates hunger

functions of pneuma.def: One of the seven factors of existence of the body, which serves as a vehicle for different faculties of the body and produces and maintains the innate heat of the body organs responsible for the life of individual parts

functions of arteries.def: Simple organs having movements of expansion and contraction and intermediary pause, transmitting blood and vital pneuma from the heart to the whole body

functions of flesh.def: Simple organ filling gaps between other simple organs, protecting underlying structures from external injuries and serving specialized functions according to its distribution in the body

waste material(s) of body.def: Materials which are not required in the body and need to be excreted, e.g., urine, excrement, sweat, etc.

tear.def: Loss of continuity of muscle at its end

sweat.def: This term does not cover ‘Araq, mentioned under Unani pharmacy

milk.def: This term does not cover Laban, mentioned under Unani pharmacology

moderation of vital pneuma.def: The process of moderating the heat of the vital pneuma through respiration

inspiration.def: Process of being mentally stimulated to use some drugs for a particular disease

pathology.def: The study of disease, its etiology, disease processes and mechanisms

cause(s).def: (1) Occurring first and thus necessitating the existence or persistence of a certain state of the human body; (2) anything which affects, assists, maintains or imparts a function, whether actively or passively

the study of causes.def: The study and knowledge of causes of health and disease

pathological causes.def: Causes which are specifically responsible for creating pathological lesions / diseases

physical (bodily) causes / corporeal cau.def: Causes related to the body itself

conjoined causes / interdependent causes.def: Interdependent bodily causes, e.g., sepsis in fever

predisposing causes.def: Causes that affect the body from within (repletion, starvation)

extracorporeal causes/ external causes/.def: Causes that affect the body from outside (trauma, cold, heat), noncorporeal (outside the body) causes which produce corporeal states directly, e.g., blow, atmospheric heat, hot or cold food or psychological factors like

intrinsic causes/ essential causes.def: Essential causes,usually intrinsic in nature and not dependent on other causes

dependent causes.def: Causes that are dependent on other causes

consequential causes.def: Causes which, when abolished, have effects that persist

nonconsequential causes.def: Causes whose effects disappear when the causes are abolished

ancillary causes.def: Causes which are not basic in nature but arise out of certain basic causes

disfiguring causes standard terminologie.def: Causes that disfigure the shape of an organ Description

causes of obstruction/ embolus.def: Causes responsible for obstruction, possibly inflammation/swelling of tract or presence of a foreign body

causes of constriction of tract/duct.def: Causes responsible for narrowing of tract, possibly inflammation/swelling of tract or presence of a foreign body

causes of dilatation of tract / duct.def: Causes responsible for dilatation of tract, possibly weakening of circular constricting fibres, increased eliminating power or drugs that dilate the tract temporarily

surface-roughening causes.def: Causes responsible for roughness of the surface, including the use of detergent, resolvent, dry or cold medicines, etc.

causes of smoothness.def: Causes responsible for smoothness of surface of skin or mucous membrane, including use of demulcent substances, mild resolvent medicines, and mild hot medicines that normalize the coldness of the surface

causes of dislocation and displacement.def: Causes responsible for dislocation or displacement of organs, including increased tension/pressure in a joint, rigorous movement, or any condition that loosens joint/muscle, etc.

causes of abnormal association between o.def: Causes responsible for abnormal association, such as stiffness, spasm, flaccidity of adjacent organs or a foreign body affecting organ in contact Description

causes of abnormal movements.def: Causes responsible for abnormal movement of an organ, such as weakness, dryness, spasm or collection of morbid material in the organ

causes of increased size of organ.def: Causes responsible for increase in size include increased quantity of material due to increased absorption in the organ

causes of increase in number of organs.def: Causes responsible for increase in number usually involve malformation

causes of decreased size of organ.def: Causes responsible for a decrease in the size of organs may involve malformation, accidental loss or decay due to infection, etc.

causes of decreased number of organ.def: Causes responsible for a decrease in number of organs may be malformation, accidental loss or decay due to infection

state-modifying causes.def: Factors modifying the state of the human body

causes of loss of continuity.def: Causes of loss of continuity of organs include internal causes like corrosive humour (as caseation and loss of tissue in leprosy) or excessive abnormal humour (accumulated in joints in arthritis), or external causes, pos

causes of ulcer standard terminologies o.def: Causes of ulcer formation may be bursting of an inflammatory mass, pustule or abscess, pus formation in an injury, etc. Description

causes of inflammation.def: Causes of inflammation may be strong expulsive power of an adjacent organ and weak retentive power of the affected organ; the causes of inflammation of an organ may vary depending on the nature of the organ, its texture,

causes of pain.def: Causes of pain include sudden and temporary change in temperament or loss of continuity

causes that relieve pain / pain alleviat.def: Causes that relieve pain include removal of the cause of pain or increase in moistness, inducing sleep, and the use of soothing and analgesic agents

causes of pleasure.def: Causes of pleasure include sudden restoration of abnormal temperament and restoration of loss of continuity; causes of pleasure here are in a context of pain reversal

causes of retention.def: Weak expulsive faculty, strong retentive faculty which may result due to weakness of the digestive faculty, narrowing and obstruction of passages and thickness, viscidity and increased amount of waste material Descriptio

causes of evacuation.def: Strong expulsive faculty, weak retentive faculty, matter causing injury by its excessive quantity which produces heaviness, gaseous substances, distension, corrosive and acrid matter, dilution of matter and extended pass

causes of indigestion and repletion.def: These include weakness of the digestive faculty, weak evacuation power, strong retentive power, narrowing of ducts and channels, Ḥammām (Turkish bath/bath) after meal, excess of repose and lack of exercise and movement

causes of weakness of organs.def: Causes of weakness of organs may include persistent abnormal temperament, especially cold abnormal temperament, weakness of texture of organs, especially nerves, weakness of different faculties and presence of compound d

heat-producing causes / calorifacients /.def: Agents/substances or procedures which increase the metabolism of the body due to their hot temperament or heat-producing property, e.g., moderate diet, exercise, movement, massage, cupping without scarification

cooling agents / refrigerants.def: Agents/substances or procedures which reduce body temperature from normal limits, e.g., excessive and prolonged movement, excessive rest, excessive intake of food

moistness-producing causes / moistening.def: Agents/substances or procedures which humidify/increase the moisture in the body, like sleeping, using wet substances, etc. Description

dryness-producing causes / desiccants /.def: Agents/substances or procedures which produce dryness in the body, e.g., excessive movement, sleeplessness, excessive evacuation, excess of psychic movements, etc.

states of the body.def: Various states of the body, including state of disease, state of health and an intermediate state

healthy state.def: A state which helps to maintain the functions of the human body through proper balancing of its temperament and composition

intermediate state.unani: Lā Ṣiḥḥat Lā Maraḍ / Ḥālat Thālitha
intermediate state.def: An intermediate state in which there is neither absolute health nor absolute disease

state of disease.def: The abnormal condition or unnatural state of the human body due to abnormal temperament or abnormal composition that leads to functional disorder as a primary consequence

simple disease.def: Simple disease is produced either due to abnormality of temperament or composition or breach in continuity
